Angela Erdmann never knew her grandfather. He died in 1946, six years before she was born. But, on Tuesday, April 8, 2014, she told of the surprising moment when she got a message in a bottle, 101 years after he put it into the Baltic Sea. Thought to be the world’s oldest message in a bottle, it was given to Erdmann by the museum that is now showing it in Germany. “It was very surprising,” Angela Erdmann, 62, said, remembering how she found out about the bottle. “A man stood at my door and told me he had mail from my grandfather. He then told me that a message in a bottle had been found and that the name on the card was my grandfather’s.” Her visitor was a family researcher who had managed to find her in Berlin after the letter was sent to the International Maritime Museum in the northern port city of Hamburg. The brown beer bottle, which had been in the water for 101 years, was found in the catch of Konrad Fischer, a fisherman, who had been in the Baltic Sea near the northern city of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a worker for ocean and science at the museum, said this bottled message was the oldest he had found. “There are papers that have been found without the bottle that are older and are in the museum,” he said. “But, with the bottle and the paper, this is certainly the oldest at the moment. It is in extremely good condition.”

Police and intelligence groups around the world have, for almost 100 years, used polygraphs as lie detectors to help catch criminals or find spies and traitors. The polygraph is popular in movies, with many exciting scenes showing the guilty sweating heavily while they are connected. But this invention could soon be out of use. Researchers in Britain and the Netherlands have made a breakthrough, creating a method with a success rate in tests of over 70% that could be in use in police stations around the world in the next ten years. Instead of relying on facial twitches, talking too much, or waving arms – all seen as signs of lying – the new method looks at full-body motions to show signs of guilty feelings. The polygraph is widely used in the US in criminal cases and for security checks for the FBI and CIA, but it is much less common in Europe. Many people in the scientific and legal fields doubt its reliability. In contrast, the new method developed by researchers has done well in tests. The main idea is that liars move around more, so the use of a full-body motion suit – like those used in films to create computer characters – will catch this. One of the research team, Ross Anderson, said: “Decades of deception research show that the interviewer will tell truth from lies only slightly better than random.” He said the new method, on the other hand, got a reliability rate of over 70%. In some tests, the team has already reached more than 80%. Anderson said: “The message is that guilty people move around more, and we can measure this strongly.” The polygraph was created in 1921 by policeman John Larson, based on research by psychologist William Marston. It records changes in pulse, blood pressure, sweating, and breathing to find out if a subject is lying. While movies suggest the device is almost perfect, the US Supreme Court said in 1998 that there was no agreement that the polygraph was reliable, a finding also backed by the US National Academy of Scientists in 2003.

Do you want your child to be good at sports, make the school team, and maybe one day even compete in the world? Well, try to make sure that your future Olympian or World Cup winner is born in November or, if not, in October. A study has found that school children born in those months are fitter than the others in their class. Children born in November and October are fitter, stronger, and more powerful than their friends born in the other ten months, especially those whose birthdays are in April or June. Dr. Gavin Sandercock, who is from the Center for Sports and Exercise Science at Essex University and led the study, found that autumn-born children had “a clear physical advantage” over their classmates. The research involved 8,550 boys and girls aged between ten and 16 from 26 state schools in Essex. All were tested between 2007 and 2010 on three different measures of fitness: stamina, handgrip strength, and lower-body power. The results showed that a child's month of birth could make “important” differences to their levels of heart fitness, muscle strength, and ability to speed up, all of which are necessary for being good at sports. Performances at school sports days in the weeks ahead may show these findings. November-born children were the fittest overall as they had the most stamina and power and were the second strongest. Those born in October were almost as fit, scoring highest for strength and coming third for power, with December children close behind. The difference in strength between children in the same class but born in different months was sometimes very large. “For example, we found that a boy born in November can run at least 10% faster, jump 12% higher, and is 15% more powerful than a child of the same age born in April. This is, potentially, a huge physical advantage,” said Sandercock. Such gaps could decide who becomes a top-level athlete because, as the paper says, “selection into elite sports may often depend on very small differences in a person’s physical performance”. The study, published in the International Journal of Sports Medicine, found that, when scores for the three kinds of fitness were combined, those born in April were the least fit, then those in June. This could lead to those children being left out from school teams and becoming less successful in sports, Sandercock said.

The small space is made to seem like a classroom. Its iron walls have educational charts – pictures of letters and a map of Bangladesh. But, the sound of hammering and the chemicals in the air that catch in the throat and irritate the eyes make it hard to focus. The children who learn in this three-square-meter room are the lucky ones. They have got away from working in the factories across the street. For 14 years, SOHAY, a grassroots group funded by the Global Fund for Children and Comic Relief, has worked in the slum areas of Dhaka to get child workers into school. It focuses on children working in dangerous conditions – in aluminum and plastic factories, and tanneries. The classroom is one of 23 urban centers that SOHAY has made in the capital. These centers help children get ready for primary school with classes that help them catch up on their studies. Once they are in primary school, the children can do homework at the centers, with help from their friends. Alamin, ten, who used to work in a plastic factory, goes to one of the centers. His father is a street seller and his mother works part-time. They are all happy that he is now in school and away from dangerous work. His friend Rabi says he wants to forget his past in the factory. “I like school,” he says. SOHAY also runs meetings for parents and employers to stop child labor and offers skills training to help family income. “Getting working children into school is really very hard,” says SOHAY’s program manager, Mohammed al-Mamun. “Their mind and body are not like other children in society. After they leave work, they sometimes find it hard to make friends and adjust to school.” It is also very hard to make sure they stay in school – the dropout amount is very high for these children. Seven-year-old Zhorna Akter Sumayya has two older brothers, both of whom have jobs (one at a restaurant, one at a local club). But, after being shown education at one of SOHAY’s centers, she now goes to a state primary school. Her parents can’t live without the money their sons bringhome, but they want their daughter to go to school.

A big fight over the right of Bolivia's native tribes to chew coca leaves, the main part of cocaine, has come up, with many effects for world drugs policy. Bolivia has got a special exemption from the 1961 Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, which is the plan for world drugs policy, letting its native people chew the leaves. Bolivia argued that the convention goes against its new rules, made in 2009, which say it must “protect native and ancestral coca as cultural treasure” and claims that coca “in its natural state ... is not a dangerous drug.” South American tribes have chewed coca leaves for many years. The leaves are said to give energy and have healing qualities. People who support Bolivia's view praised it for standing up for native rights. “The Bolivian move is inspiring and new,” said Danny Kushlick, Head of External Affairs at the Transform Drug Policy Foundation, which pushes for drug freedom. “It shows that any nation that has had enough of the war on drugs can change its part with the UN rules.” But the UN’s International Narcotics Control Board (INCB), which looks after the global drug rules, has told Bolivia it might hurt the world drug control system. Several nations – including the UK, the US, Italy, Sweden, the Netherlands, and Russia – did not agree with Bolivia’s wants. The UK’s report to the UN, which watches over the convention, said it “knows and respects the cultural value of the coca leaf in Bolivia,” but it added: “The United Kingdom is … worried that this exemption could lead to more coca growing and – most importantly – to more coca going into the cocaine trade. Because of this, the exemption would hurt world law in relation to the global fight against the illegal drugs trade and could hurt the world's response to that trade.”

On Black Fell, looking down on Northumberland’s good Kielder Water reservoir, a group of people wait in a car park next to a strange wood building with a simple design from the future. This is Kielder Observatory, the center of Britain’s new astrotourism industry. And those waiting outside are the lucky ones. Many more have asked for a night of stargazing at the observatory, but numbers are strictly limited. Inside, next to a wood burner and under low lights, the observatory’s leader and main astronomer, Gary Fildes, a former bricklayer with wild hair, gives a talk to his team. The group talks about the chance of seeing the northern lights, but Fildes is not sure. Instead, they decide to train their strong telescopes on Jupiter and Venus and later to spot stars like Capella and Betelgeuse. An extra attraction is the sight of the International Space Station. “Remember,” Fildes tells his team, “it’s about interaction, it’s about fun, it’s about inspiring people.” Fildes, 49, is at the front of the UK’s growing astrotourism industry. The key moment for Northumberland came in 2013 when the whole national park with Hadrian’s Wall, along with Kielder Water and Forest Park, about 1,500 square kilometers, was given Dark Sky Park status, the only one in England. Dark Sky Parks are rare. The 2013 Star Count showed that only 5% of the UK people can see more than 31 stars on a good night. The Tucson-based International Dark-Sky Association (IDA) gives this status only to places that take big steps to avoid light pollution. Recipients must also show their night skies are dark enough. In Northumberland Dark Sky Park, as the area was renamed, it is so dark that Venus makes a shadow on the Earth. Duncan Wise, visitor development officer for the Northumberland National Park Authority, helped to lead the campaign for dark-sky status. “We tend to look at landscape as everything up to the horizon,” Wise said. “But what about what’s above it?” Wise and others spent years making their submission to the IDA, collecting many light readings and forming a group of local councils, parks’ bodies, and community groups to create a lighting plan that influences the building of new places in the area. Their work has been shown to be right. Many of the 1.5 million who visit Northumberland each year now know of its Dark Sky status. “We get a lot of people coming here to see the sky now,” says the man at the car-hire firm in Newcastle. “They come in autumn and winter, when it’s darkest. Good for the local hotels as they get business all year round now.”

When Larry Pizzi first heard about electric bikes almost 20 years ago, he asked: “Why would anyone want to change a bike by putting a motor and batteries on it?” This is a question that still confuses traditionalists. Bike shops have been slow to stock e-bikes, even though they have been here since the late 1990s. Sales in the US have been small. Pizzi, who is now the leader of Currie Technologies, the top seller of e-bikes in the US, believes that this is about to change. Others in the business agree. Well-known brands like Trek, Raleigh, and Specialized all offer electric models and they are betting the market is about to grow. “We’re on the edge of popular use,” Pizzi said. “There are more players entering the group, it seems, with every month.” The US is different when it comes to electric bikes. Almost 32 million e-bikes were sold in 2014, mostly in China, where they are mainly used for transport. They are also popular in much of Europe. They are common in the Netherlands and Switzerland; German postal workers use them to move around and BMW offers one for about $3,000. Electric bikes are different from motorcycles or mopeds, which rely on motor power; they are bikes that can be pedaled with – or without – help from an electric motor. Riding an e-bike feels like riding a normal bike with a strong tailwind; the motor helps you go faster or climb hills but it’s usually not the main source of power. Unlike mopeds, e-bikes are often allowed on bike paths and they can’t go faster than 20 mph.

Such is the life of the modern chemist: you wait a long time for a new element to appear, and then four come together all at once. Found by workers in Japan, Russia, and the US, the four new elements are the first added to the periodic table since 2011, when elements 114 and 116 were added. The new elements, all very short-lived and highly radioactive, complete the seventh row of the periodic table and make science books worldwide out of date. The US-based International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the group that controls chemical names, terms, and measures, confirmed the elements on 30 December, 2015, after looking over studies from 2004. The scientists who found them must now create official names to replace the awkward Latin-based names – ununtrium, ununpentium, ununseptium, and ununoctium – which show their atomic numbers, 113, 115, 117, and 118. The atomic number is the number of protons in an element’s core. IUPAC said that a Russian-American group of scientists at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California had gathered enough proof to claim the discovery of elements 115, 117, and 118. The group given credit for discovering element 113, which had also been claimed by the Russians and Americans, was a team from the RIKEN Institute in Japan. This decision means Japan is the first Asian country to name an element. Under IUPAC rules, new elements can be named after mythological ideas, minerals, a place or country, or a scientist.

Covering a car with a top speed of 25mph, two seats, and no pedals or wheel may not make much of a show at a motor event. But Google, in the US, sent a small shake through the car and taxi fields as it showed the latest form of its driverless car. The electric vehicle, which Google has started testing around its base in Mountain View, California, gets rid of all the normal controls, including foot pedals. Instead, it has a smartphone app that calls it and tells it where to go, and a single STOP button placed between the two front seats in case the people inside need to take over from the computer. The car takes care of all the work of navigation, steering, speeding up, and stopping. The company is making about 100 models for a two-year test. The company’s co-founder, Sergey Brin, told a meeting in California that the vehicle was “still in the model stage” but that the project was “about changing the world for people who are not served well by transport today.” He said of the car: “You’re just sitting there; no wheel, no pedals. For me, it was very relaxing. About ten seconds after getting in, I forgot I was there. It reminded me of catching a chairlift alone – a bit of quiet I found really nice.” Google says that the main aim of the project is to make safety better and that, because the car is made with soft foam at the front and a plastic screen, “it should be far safer than any other car for people on foot.” The cars, which have been made specially by a company (not yet named) in Detroit, will be used to look further into how to make driverless vehicles work best. Google will now run a program to test the cars more, which are not yet for sale.

Galina Zaglumyonova was woken in her flat in central Chelyabinsk by a big explosion that broke the balcony windows and smashed clay pots with her few houseplants. When she jumped out of bed, she could see a huge vapor trail in the morning sky and hear the sound of car alarms from the street below. “I didn’t understand what was going on,” said Zaglumyonova. “There was a big explosion and then a series of little explosions. My first thought was that it was a plane crash.” What she saw were the last moments of a ten-ton meteorite that came to Earth in a series of fireballs just after sunrise. Officials said almost 1,200 people were hurt, with more than 40 taken to hospital – most because of flying glass broken by the sound made as the meteorite came down. There were no reported deaths. The meteorite entered the air traveling at a speed of at least 33,000 mph and broke into pieces between 18 and 32 miles above the ground, according to a statement from the Russian Academy of Sciences. The event caused fear in Chelyabinsk, a Russian city of one million people south of the Ural mountains, as mobile phone networks quickly became busy with calls. Amateur video from the area, often filled with the bad language of scared viewers, showed the pieces of the meteorite shining more brightly as they came near to hitting. The vapor trail was seen for hundreds of miles around, including in neighboring Kazakhstan. Tatyana Bets was at work in the reception area of a hospital clinic in the center of the city when the meteorite hit. “First we felt the wind, and then the room was filled with a very bright light, and we could see a cloud of some unknown smoke in the sky,” she said. Then, after a few minutes, came the explosions. At least three holes were later found, according to the Ministry of the Interior, and were being watched by the military. One hole was more than six meters wide, while another piece of meteorite was said to have crashed through the thick ice of a nearby lake. Radiation levels at the hit sites were normal, according to local military officials.

An octopus has made a bold escape from the National Aquarium in New Zealand by breaking out of its tank, sliding down a 50-meter drainpipe, and vanishing into the sea. In a scene like Finding Nemo, Inky – a common New Zealand octopus – made his run for freedom after the lid of his tank was left a little open. Staff think that in the middle of the night, while the aquarium was empty, Inky climbed to the top of his glass home, down the side of the tank, and moved across the floor of the aquarium. Rob Yarrell, national manager of the National Aquarium of New Zealand in Napier, said: “Octopuses are known as escape artists. I don’t think he was unhappy or lonely, as octopuses like to be alone. But, he is such a curious boy. He would want to know what’s happening outside. That’s just his way.” One idea is that Inky slid across the aquarium floor – a distance of three or four meters – and then, feeling freedom was close, went into a drainpipe that led straight to the sea. The drainpipe was 50 meters long and opened onto the waters of Hawke’s Bay, on the east coast of New Zealand’s North Island. Another way out could have been Inky squeezing into an open pipe at the top of his tank, which went under the floor to the drain. “When we came in the next morning and his tank was empty, I was really shocked,” said Yarrell, who has not started a search for Inky. “The staff and I have been quite sad. But then, this is Inky and he’s always been a bit of a surprise octopus.” Reiss Jenkinson, exhibits keeper at the National Aquarium, said he was very sure Inky had not been taken. “I understand how octopuses act very well,” he said. “I have seen octopuses on boats slip through pumps. And, the security here is too tight for anyone to take Inky and why would they?” Because octopuses have no bones, they can fit into very small spaces and have been seen squeezing through gaps the size of coins. They are also known to be very smart and able to use tools. At the Island Bay Marine Education Center in Wellington, an octopus was found to have a habit of visiting another tank overnight to steal crabs, then returning to its own. Another at the center, Ozymandias, was thought to have broken a world record for opening a jar before it was released into the ocean.

Crunchy, full of protein, and found under a rock near you. Insects have long been not seen as food in most places in the world – but now they are coming closer to our plates. In spring 2013, there will be a move to take away the dislike and put insects not only on special food menus but also on supermarket shelves. In April, there will be a festival in London, Pestival 2013 – an event to like insects, where eating creepy-crawlies is important. It will have a two-day “pop-up” restaurant by the Nordic Food Lab, the team from Denmark behind the restaurant Noma, which brought ants to the table for a sold-out ten-day run at Claridge’s hotel in London in 2012. In March, a BBC show will show food writer Stefan Gates looking for and eating deep-fried locusts and barbecued tarantulas. But, behind all the tricks and jokes about flies in soup, there is a serious message. Many experts believe there is a clear good for the environment if humans eat creepy-crawlies. The UN’s Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has funded projects since 2011 to promote the eating and farming of insects in southeast Asia and Africa, where about two billion people already eat insects and caterpillar larvae as a regular part of their diet. In 2012, the FAO published a list of 1,909 kinds of edible insects, and with support from the Dutch government, plans a big international meeting on “this valuable food source” in 2013. Over the next 30 years, the human population will grow to nine billion. Already one billion people do not get enough food. The growth will mean more pressure on farming land, water, forests, fisheries, and biodiversity sources, as well as nutrients and energy supplies. The cost of meat is going up, not only in money but also in the amount of rainforest destroyed for grazing or to grow feed for cattle. There is also the issue of methane from cows. The farming of livestock adds a lot to greenhouse gas emissions – 35% of the planet’s methane, 65% of its nitrous oxide, and 9% of the carbon dioxide.

The eight hours in the office are over. The most important work of the day is done; what is left can wait till morning. This is when many workers think about going to the door. Yet, for millions of Japanese workers, the thought of clearing their desks and getting home in time for dinner can bring claims of being unloyal to the company. But, after many years of letting companies take all they can from their workers, a challenge to Japan’s deep culture of overwork has come from the government, which is thinking about making it law for workers to take at least five days of paid holiday each year. Japanese workers now have a right to an average of 18.5 days of paid holiday each year – only two less than the world average – with at least ten days, plus 15 one-day national holidays. In truth, few even come close to taking all of their days, often using only nine of their 18.5-day average right. While many British workers see a two-week summer holiday as a basic right, workers in Japan have come to think of a four-night break as too much. The change, to be talked about in this parliamentary meeting, comes after companies started urging workers to nap at work to make them better. By the end of the decade, the government hopes that, if passed, the law will push Japanese workers to follow the example of British workers, who use an average of 20 days of paid holiday each year, and those in France, who take an average of 25. Japan’s hard work culture may have helped it become a strong economy, with its loyal workers praised in the world for their service to the company, but this is often at the cost of everything else. Japan’s low birth rate and predictions of a fast drop in population are partly blamed on the lack of time couples have to start families. More workers are getting sick from stress, or worse, dying from overwork. Even with studies showing that longer hours in the office or workshop do not make people more productive, today’s workers are still feeling the hangover from the bubble years of the 1980s.

A lonely old man who lives in a hole on the moon is the strange focus of John Lewis’s Christmas 2015 ad, as the store gives a charity touch to its big plan. With more talk around John Lewis’s seasonal ad, which has come to start the Christmas shopping time for many, the store will try to use its name to raise hundreds of thousands of pounds for the charity Age UK. It will also ask staff and customers to join their local group of the charity to help old people who may be alone during the holiday. The shop has spent £7m on a plan that goes from the smooth TV ad to a phone game and goods, including glow-in-the-dark pyjamas, and areas made to look like the moon’s surface in 11 shops. After two years of successful ads with cute animals – a bear and a hare, then a penguin – this time, the shop is pulling on the heartstrings with a story of a young girl, Lily, who sees an old man living in a shack on the moon through her telescope. The brave child tries to send him a letter and shoot a note with a bow and arrow, before sending him a present of a telescope tied to balloons, which helps them make contact. The ad says: “Show someone they are loved this Christmas,” which matches Age UK’s own message: “No one should have no one at Christmas.” Profits from three items – a mug, gift tag, and card – will go to the charity. “The charity really connects with people at this time of year and the ad helps in thinking about someone who lives near you and may not see anyone,” said Rachel Swift, head of marketing at John Lewis. The plan has the Oasis song Half the World Away sung by the little-known Norwegian artist Aurora. The ad cost £1m to make. The moon scenes were shot at the Warner Bros Studios, where the Harry Potter films were made, and the special set was made by one of the team behind the latest Star Wars film, The Force Awakens. As in the last few years, John Lewis has raised interest in their latest ad before its release with a teaser plan on TV and social media using the tag #OnTheMoon. There will be a full moon on Christmas Day 2015 – a complete chance, according to Swift.

Nasal spray with the ‘love chemical’ oxytocin may help children with autism get better at social situations, US researchers say. Scans of children with autism showed that one dose of this chemical improved brain actions to facial looks, a change that could make social talks feel more easy and rewarding for them. The scientists said a course of oxytocin might make behavioral therapies, used to help people with autism, work better. “Over time, you would expect to see more fitting social responses, more interest in talking with other people, more eye contact, and better talking ability,” said Kevin Pelphrey, head of the Child Neuroscience Lab at Yale University.

Autism is a condition seen in more than one in 100 people. It affects people in different ways, but is marked by troubles in social talks and communication. So far, there is no good treatment for the social problems caused by autism. Researchers at Yale have looked at the brain chemical oxytocin as a possible treatment for these social troubles because it plays an important role in bonding and trust. Results have been mixed; one recent study found no big benefit for youths given the chemical over several days. But Pelphrey said oxytocin may help the brain learn from social actions; it would work best with therapies that push people with autism to engage more socially, he said. “Our study shows that oxytocin affects the brain and opens up the chance that, when used with behavioral treatments, it works like a social boost,” he said.

The scientists used a method called functional MRI to scan the brains of 17 youths aged eight to 16 with autism while they looked at pictures of cars or the eyes of people showing various feelings. The scans were done 45 minutes after the participants breathed in a placebo or oxytocin through a nasal spray. The scans showed that reward parts in the children’s brains acted more normally after a sniff of oxytocin, being more active when the person looked at faces and less active when looking at the still cars. The study appears in the latest issue of the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences in the United States.

Benjamin Carle is 96.9% made in France, all the way down to his underpants and socks. Sadly, six Ikea forks, a Chinese guitar, and paint without a source stopped him from being called a 100% economic patriot, but nobody is perfect. Carle, 26, set out in 2013 to see if it was possible to live using only French-made products for ten months as part of a television story. The idea came from the Minister for Economic Renewal Arnaud Montebourg’s call for the public to buy French to help the country’s industry. The test cost Carle his smartphone, television, refrigerator (all made in China); his glasses (Italian); his underpants (Moroccan); morning coffee (Guatemalan) and his beloved David Bowie music (British). Luckily, his patient girlfriend, Anaïs, and cat, Loon, (both French) stayed with him. “Politicians say all sorts of things and want us to go along. I wanted to see if it was possible and easy to do what the minister said; to hold him to his words,” Carle told the Guardian over a non-French coffee in a Paris café after finishing his story. He set three rules for himself: eat only food made in France, stop contact with foreign goods, and do so on €1,800 a month (above the minimum wage of €1,430 to cover the extra cost of living in Paris). The journalist was shocked to find out at the start of the test that only 4.5% of the things in his flat were made in the country – and that the rest had to go, including the light bulbs (China) and green beans (Kenya). The movers left Carle’s home nearly empty. Without a refrigerator (none are made in France) or nail clippers, he had to chill his food on the window ledge and saw at his toenails with a penknife. His foreign-made clothes, even his underwear, were replaced with more expensive options: French-made underpants (€26), socks (€9), polo shirt (€75), espadrille sandals (€26), but no jeans as none are made in France. During the test, Carle searched supermarket shelves for 100% French-made products, learned to cook seasonal fruit and vegetables grown in France, proudly brushed his teeth with the last toothbrush made in France by a company in Picardie employing 29 people, and hand-washed his smalls until he found the last French-made washing machine (which, being top opening, would not fit under the kitchen counter).

The Moroccan city of Ouarzazate is used to big works. It is at the edge of the Sahara Desert and in the center of Morocco’s “Ouallywood” film work. Now, the trading city is the place for a group of four linked solar big plants, which, with water and wind, will help give almost half of Morocco’s power from renewables by 2020, with, it is hoped, some extra to send to Europe. The project is a key part in Morocco’s wish to use its deserts to become a world solar power. When the full group is done, it will be the biggest concentrated solar power plant in the world, and the first part, called Noor 1, will start in November 2015. The mirror way it uses is less common and more costly than the solar panels that are now known on roofs all over the world, but it will have the good side of being able to keep giving power even after the sun goes down. As engineers put the last touches to Noor 1, its 500,000 crescent-shaped solar mirrors shine across the desert view. The 800 rows follow the sun as it moves across the sky, making quiet sounds every few minutes as their shadows slip further east. When they are done, the four plants at Ouarzazate will take a space as big as Morocco’s capital city, Rabat, and give 580 mega-watts (MW) of power, enough for a million homes. The power from the desert has been known for many years. In the days after the Chernobyl nuclear accident in 1986, the German physics expert Gerhard Knies worked out that the world’s deserts get enough energy in a few hours to meet humanity’s power needs for a whole year. The problem has been getting that energy and moving it to the places where it is needed. Experts think that solar power will make up a third of Morocco’s renewable energy supply by 2020, with wind and water sharing the same amount each. “We are very proud of this work,” Morocco’s environment minister, Hakima el-Haite, said. “I think it is the most important solar plant in the world.”

Tigers are more common in Nepal than at any time since the 1970s, a new count has shown, giving those who help animals hope that the big cats, whose numbers have been going down in south Asia for 100 years, can be saved. The number of wild royal bengal tigers in Nepal has gone up to 198 – a 63.6% rise in five years – the government survey said. “This is very good news,” said Maheshwar Dhakal, a worker with Nepal’s Department of National Parks and Wildlife Conservation. The count is based on looking at pictures from more than 500 cameras placed in five safe areas and three wildlife paths. More than 250 helpers and wildlife experts worked on the survey, which cost about £250,000. Rising wealth in Asia has raised prices for tiger skins and body parts used in traditional Chinese medicines. Groups from outside pay poor local Nepali large amounts to kill the cats. The skin and bones are given to middlemen, who go easily across the loose border to India, where the main dealers are. One big problem is the connection between senior workers and crime groups involved in the trade. Experts say the rise in numbers is due to better policing of national parks, stronger efforts against poaching, and better care of tiger homes in Nepal, where forests cover 29% of the land. But they say Nepal needs to do more to protect the homes and animals on which tigers feed so the big cats have enough space to move and food to eat. As the number of tigers has risen over the years, so have problems with villagers. Seven people were killed in tiger attacks around national parks in 2012 compared to four in 2011, park workers said. Villagers are also wanting better protection.

Robert Myslajek stops. Between two paw prints on a muddy mountain road, the scientist finds what he looks for. “Droppings!” he says happily. Wolf sightings are rare, so seeing their feces marks a good day, even for a skilled tracker. But it is getting easier. There are now about 1,500 wolves in Poland. The number has doubled in 15 years. Wolves are – with the brown bear, the lynx, and the wolverine – Europe’s last big meat-eating animals. Caretakers from Britain, Germany, and the Netherlands are coming here to find out how Poland has saved this protected kind, badly shown even in fairy tales. The scientist, who is well known on Polish television, says wolves are special animals that can move up to 30km during one hunt. “The Beskidy pack is strong, with eight or nine members. This year, we have seen five cubs, two yearlings, and two adults. We track them using cameras in the forest and by following their prints in the mud and snow. In each family group, only one pair has young, once a year. All pack members care for the young together.” Myslajek, the son of a shepherd, is confused by wolves’ bad name. “Why do we speak of a 'lone-wolf gunman'? Why did we need Little Red Riding Hood, with its big bad wolf?” He is intrigued by these distant canines who stayed in the wild 33,000 years ago when others chose a more comfortable life as house dogs. Wolves are not pets. Myslajek says only scientific reasons – the need to help forests grow and control the wild animal number – can save Europe’s wild meat-eaters, especially the disliked wolf. “Natural predators balance the system. They keep plant eaters in check, thus allowing trees to grow tall for birds to nest in.” The ban on wolf hunting in the western Carpathian mountains started in 1995 and nationwide in Poland in 1998. There are now packs in almost all the country’s big forests. The predators live with humans instead of being kept away, as they are in African safari parks.

Illegal download is a kind of “moral dirt” and theft, as much as reaching into someone’s pocket and taking their wallet is theft, says writer Philip Pullman. In an article for Index on Censorship, Pullman, who is head of the Society of Authors, makes a strong defence of copyright laws. He is very critical of Internet users who think it is OK to download music or books without paying for them. “The technical smartness is so bright that people can’t see the moral dirt of what they’re doing,” he writes. “It is wrong that anyone can take an artist’s work and get away with it. It is theft, as surely as reaching into someone’s pocket and taking their wallet is theft.” His article comes after music industry leaders met British Prime Minister David Cameron in Downing Street, where the issue of web stealing was discussed. Pullman, writer of the His Dark Materials story, says authors and musicians work in poverty and shadow for years to bring their work to the level “that gives joy to their audiences and, as soon as they achieve that, the chance of making a living from it is taken away from them.” He concludes: “The idea is simple, and not changed by technology, science or magic: if we want to enjoy the work that someone does, we should pay for it.” Pullman is writing in the next issue of the campaign group’s magazine in a talk with Cathy Casserly, head of Creative Commons, which offers open content licenses “that lets creators take copyright into their own hands.” Casserly argues that there is much wrong with copyright, which was made “in a past age.” Casserly writes: “By default, copyright shuts the door on many ways that people can share, build on and remix each other’s work, chances that were impossible when those laws were made.” She says artists need to think smartly about how they spread and earn from their work, quoting the science fiction writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My problem is not stealing, it’s being unknown.”

From all over Rwanda, and even parts of close Burundi, people go to the southern town of Butare to a small shop called Inzozi Nziza (Sweet Dreams). They come for a taste of the unknown, something most have not tasted – the sweet, cold, soft feeling of ice cream. Here, at the first ice cream shop in the central African country, customers can buy scoops in sweet cream, passion fruit, strawberry, and pineapple flavors. Toppings include fresh fruit, honey, chocolate bits, and granola. Black tea and coffee are also on sale. The shop, which has “ice cream, coffee, dreams” on its sign, is catching local interest about the dessert – and “changing lives” in the process, says Inzozi Nziza’s manager, Louise Ingabire. “Ice cream is important,” she says between bites of a honey-flavored scoop. “Some Rwandans like ice cream, but it’s a new thing. We still have some work to do, to tell others that they’ll enjoy it.” True to Inzozi Nziza’s saying, the shop can certainly make dreams come true. “I didn’t have a job before: I just stayed at home. Now, I have a vision for the future. I am making money and I can give some of it to my family,” says the 27-year-old. Butare, which has 89,600 people and is 135km south of the capital, Kigali, is the home of the National University of Rwanda. Inzozi Nziza has become a center for tired students looking to treat themselves to something cool and different. “It’s something bringing people together here,” Kalisa Migendo, a 24-year-old student of farming, says. “If you need to go out and talk to a friend, a girl or a boy, you come to Inzozi Nziza for an ice cream.” Most of the ingredients are from local places and the milk comes from a storage in nearby Nyanza. The vanilla beans and cocoa are brought in from other countries.

They call him the Robin Hood of the banks, a man who got many loans, worth almost half a million euros, with no aim to ever pay them back. Instead, Enric Duran gave the money to projects that made and supported choices to capitalism. After 14 months in hiding, Duran is not sorry, even though his actions could land him in jail. “I’m proud of what I did,” he said in a talk by Skype from a secret place. From 2006 to 2008, Duran got 68 business and personal loans from 39 banks in Spain. He sent the money to social workers, paying for speaking tours against capitalism and TV cameras for a media group. “I saw that, on one side, these social movements were making choices but that they lacked money and ways to speak,” he said. “Meanwhile, our economy was always growing, which led to a system that made money out of nothing.” The loans Duran took from banks were his way of changing and showing this situation, he said. He started slowly. “I filled out a few loan forms with my real details. They said no, but I just wanted to get a feel for what they were asking for.” From there, the former table-tennis coach began to build a complex web of accounts, payments and transfers. “I was learning all the time.” By the summer of 2007, he had found how to make the system work, asking for loans under the name of a false TV production company. “Then, I managed to get a lot.” €492,000, to be exact.

DNA taken from the wisdom tooth of a European hunter-gatherer has given scientists a very new look at people before farming started. The Mesolithic man, who lived in Spain about 7,000 years ago, had a strange mix of blue eyes, black or brown hair, and dark skin, according to studies of his genes. He was probably unable to digest milk well and had more trouble digesting starchy food than the farmers who changed diets and ways of living when they began to use tools in the first farming time. The start of farming brought humans and animals much closer together, and people likely grew stronger immune systems to fight off illnesses that the animals gave them. But scientists may have thought too much about the effect farming had on the human immune system, because tests on the hunter-gatherer’s DNA found that he already had changes that help the immune system tackle many bad germs. Some of these still exist in modern Europeans today. “Before we started this work, I had some ideas of what we would find,” said Carles Lalueza-Fox, who led the study at the Institute of Evolutionary Biology in Barcelona. “Most of those ideas turned out to be completely wrong.” The Spanish team began their work after a group of cavers found two skeletons in a deep and complex cave system high up in the Cantabrian Mountains of northwest Spain in 2006. The human remains, which belonged to two men in their early 30s, were very well kept by the cool air of the cave. Carbon dating put the remains at around 7,000 years old, before farming had spread into Europe from the Middle East. The timing matched with old artifacts found at the place, including reindeer teeth that had holes and were hung from the people’s clothing.

Agios Efstratios is very far and very forgotten by the banks, the government, and most of the modern world that the mobile phone network can’t process data, and there isn’t a single ATM or credit-card machine on the island. Before Greece fell into money chaos, people of this calm place in the north Aegean did quite well. They did their banking at the post office, and the few rooms for rent were full every summer with people who had heard – by word of mouth – of its great empty beaches, clear seas, and fresh fish. But, because the island still runs on cash, the closing of banks across the country has been very bad. Residents have had to take nine-hour trips to the nearest big island to get cash, and Greek visitors say they can’t get enough cash to come. “Visitor numbers are down 80% this year,” said Mayor Maria Kakali, in an office in the village where she grew up, which is home to about 200 people. “Even people born here and living in Athens, who have their own places on the island, aren’t coming.” Kakali has pushed the government and a big Greek bank to promise an ATM within weeks, but she still feels it may come too late for this season on an island where tourism is much bigger than the two other ways to earn income: fishing and farming: “We have almost no bookings in August, when usually we have people calling us for a room and we can’t help them.” A hard winter ahead may be a bit better with 50 workers staying in the village to build up the harbor, but there is a bigger crisis coming because the government has said it will end a long tax break for islands.

Every month in the UK, about 50,000 mystery shopping trips happen, according to the Mystery Shopping Providers Association. Because more and more buying is taking place online, the need for mystery shoppers is going up. Mystery shoppers act like customers in a store, but they really gather information about how good or bad the service is. “Retailers are more aware that customers who go to a physical store want a service and experience they can’t find online,” says Simon Boydell, a spokesman for Marketforce, which has over 300,000 mystery shoppers. “Our clients want to know how well their stores give that experience.”

“We give different store places to each shopper and change them, so they do not go back to the same shop in three months,” says Jill Spencer from ABa, a mystery shopping company. “Each day, they usually spend up to eight hours visiting five to ten stores and another hour or two writing detailed reports on every part of their visit.” For this, mystery shoppers can earn up to £155 a day. They also get paid back for their gas and hotel stays, and are paid for their car wear and tear (shoppers can drive up to 20,000 miles a year). 

Meanwhile, video mystery shoppers, who tape their visits with a hidden camera in a buttonhole or handbag, can earn even more – about £300 a day. Shoppers usually get back any money they spend in stores. “I typically get between £5 and £20 to spend at each store to check the service I get at the counter,” says Laura, a 50-year-old mystery shopper from Devon, who has been paid to visit around 7,000 shops since 2001. The buy usually has to be related to a service or a type of product that the retailer wants her to check. “I always get a scenario, like buying something from a specific department or a new product range, but, within that, I can often buy whatever I want – and keep it.”

Vienna is the best city in the world to live in, Baghdad is the worst, and London, Paris, and New York do not even get into the top 35, according to research about quality of life. German-speaking cities are at the top in the 18th Mercer Quality of Life study, with Vienna along with Zurich, Munich, Dusseldorf, and Frankfurt in the top seven. Paris has gone down the list, falling ten places to 37th, just before London at 39th, mostly because of the city's danger from terrorist attacks. The study looked at social and economic conditions, health, education, housing, and the environment, and is used by big companies to decide where to put offices and factories and how much to pay staff. Helena Hartlauer, 32, who was born in Vienna, said she was not surprised at her city’s top place. The local social democratic government has a long tradition of investing in high-quality social housing, making Vienna almost unique in being affordable among big cities. “I live in a 100-square-meter apartment from the turn of the century in a good area about 20 minutes’ walk from the city center. But my rent is just €800 (£625) a month.” An apartment like that in London would cost over £2,000 and even more in New York, which is ranked 44th in the list. US cities do not do well in the study, mostly because of issues around personal safety and crime. The best city in the US is San Francisco, at 28th; Boston is 34th. Canadian cities, led by Vancouver, are much better than their US rivals in the list. “You don’t see how safe Vienna is until you go abroad,” said Hartlauer. “We also have great public transport, with the underground working 24 hours at weekends, and it only costs €1 per trip.”

Clay Cockrell is sitting in his office at Columbus Circle, across the street from 1 Central Park West, which is home to Trump International Hotel and Tower. In front of the tower is Central Park, where Cockrell holds his popular walk and talk therapy sessions. Cockrell, a former Wall Street worker turned therapist, spends much of his days walking through Central Park or Battery Park in downtown Manhattan near Wall Street, as a friend and guide to some of New York’s richest people. “I moved towards it naturally,” he said of his becoming an expert in wealth help. “Many of the very wealthy – the 1% of the 1% – feel that their troubles are really not troubles. But they are. A lot of therapists do not give enough weight to their issues.” So, what troubles are America’s 1% dealing with? “There is guilt over being rich in the first place,” he said. “There is the feeling that they have to hide the fact that they are rich. And, then, there is the loneliness – being in the 1%, it turns out, can be lonely.” It seems F. Scott Fitzgerald was right: the very rich “are different from you and me.” Counselors argue things have become worse since the money crisis and the talk over income gap that has been raised by movements like Occupy Wall Street and the Fight for $15 fair wage plan. “The Occupy Wall Street movement was a good one and had some important things to say about income gap but it singled out the 1% and painted them globally as something bad,” said Jamie Traeger-Muney, a wealth psychologist and founder of the Wealth Legacy Group. The media, she said, is partly to blame for making the rich “feel like they need to hide or feel shame.” “Sometimes, I am shocked by things that people say. You would never talk about another group of people in the way that it seems normal to talk about wealth holders.”

Swedish jails have a long reputation around the world for being forward-thinking. But are the jails in the country an easy choice? The head of Sweden’s jail and probation service, Nils Oberg, said in November 2013 that four Swedish jails are to be closed because of an “out of the ordinary” fall in prisoner numbers. Although there has been no drop in crime rates, between 2011 and 2012 there was a 6% reduction in Sweden’s prisoner population, now just over 4,500. A similar fall is expected in 2013 and 2014. Oberg said he was puzzled by the unexpected decrease but expressed hope that the reason was about how his jails are run. “We certainly hope that the efforts we put into rehabilitation and preventing crime return has had an effect,” he said. “The modern jail service in Sweden is very different from when I joined as a young jail officer in 1978,” says Kenneth Gustafsson, head of Kumla Prison, Sweden’s most secure jail. However, he doesn’t think the system has become easy. “When I joined, the focus was much on humanity in jails. Prisoners were treated well – maybe too well, some might say. But, after a few high-profile escapes in 2004, we had to make changes to make jails more secure.” The Swedes still managed to keep a generally humane approach to sentences, even for the most serious offenders: jail terms rarely go over ten years. Sweden was the first country in Europe to introduce electronic tagging for convicted criminals and continues to work to reduce short jail terms as much as possible by using community punishments, which have been shown to be more effective at lowering reoffending. The overall reoffending rate in Sweden stands between 30 and 40% over three years – to compare with another European country, the number is around half that in the UK. One likely reason for the relatively low reoffending rate and the low rate of incarceration in Sweden (below 70 per 100,000 people) is that the age of criminal responsibility is set at 15. In the UK, for example, children aged ten to 17 and young people under the age of 21 have the highest reoffending rates: almost three quarters and two thirds, respectively. A good number of these offenders go on to fill adult jails. In Sweden, no young person under the age of 21 can be given a life sentence – this is not the case in many other countries – and every effort is made to ensure that as few young offenders as possible end up in jail.

Many of us know we do not get enough sleep, but think about what it would be like if we could fix it with a simple way: getting up later. In a talk at the British Science Festival, Dr. Paul Kelley, a research worker at the Sleep and Circadian Neuroscience Institute at Oxford University, asked schools to change their start times to match the natural body rhythms of their students. This would help with thinking ability, test scores, and students’ health (not having enough sleep is linked to diabetes, depression, being overweight, and a weak immune system). 

This follows a paper from 2014, where he noted that when children are about ten, their natural wake-up time is around 6:30am; at 16, it goes up to 8am; and at 18, someone you might think of as a lazy teenager actually wakes up at 9am. The usual school start time is fine for 10-year-olds but not for those aged 16 to 18. For older teenagers, it may be smarter to begin the school day at 11am or even later. “A 7am alarm for older teens,” Kelley and his team said in the paper, “is like a 4:30am start for a teacher in their 50s.” 

He mentions it is not just about getting teenagers to sleep earlier. “The body’s natural rhythm is ruled by a special kind of light,” Kelley says. “The eye does not just have rods and cones; it has cells that send information to the suprachiasmatic nuclei in the hypothalamus.” This part of the brain controls our body rhythms over a 24-hour time period. It’s like asking: ‘Why can’t you control your heartbeat?’
